title: Malt loaf
subtitle: Dense, sticky, and rich with malt syrup
excerpt: The British malt loaf: a dark, chewy, almost fudgy loaf made with malt extract, black treacle, and sultanas. It improves wrapped for a day after baking and keeps well for a week.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, the basic malt-enriched bread method. The commercial Soreen-style malt loaf is a twentieth-century development; the form here follows earlier British baking tradition, cross-referenced with interwar recipe columns in the British Newspaper Archive.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice and freeze wrapped portions. Thaw at room temperature for 30 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the day before and wrap tightly in cling film. The flavour deepens overnight.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven. Conventional 170°C. Low and slow keeps the loaf moist.

glossaryTerms.definition: A thick, sweet syrup made from malted barley. In bread baking it contributes fermentable sugars for the yeast, a characteristic malt flavour, and a sticky, chewy crumb texture. It also colours the loaf a deep brown. Available in health food shops and larger supermarkets, often sold in jars. Black treacle is the closest flavour substitute, though it is more bitter and lacks the malt character.

content.text: A British malt loaf is nothing like a standard bread. The crumb is dense, almost fudgy, and the crust has no crunch to speak of. It is made with

and black treacle alongside the flour, which gives the loaf its colour and its characteristic sticky character. One 900 g loaf, ready to bake in an hour once the dough has risen. Wrap it warm and leave overnight before cutting; the texture tightens and the flavour deepens.

Gently warm the malt extract, treacle, and milk together in a small saucepan until the malt dissolves into the milk and the temperature reaches around 38 to 40°C; it should feel warm on the inside of your wrist, not hot. Too hot will kill the yeast.

Combine the flour, sugar, salt, and yeast in a large bowl. Add the butter, then pour in the warm malt mixture. Mix until the dough comes together, then turn onto a lightly oiled surface and knead for 8 to 10 minutes, until the dough is smooth and slightly tacky. It will be stickier than a standard bread dough; resist the temptation to add more flour, as the stickiness comes from the malt sugars, not from lack of gluten development. Fold the sultanas in during the last 2 minutes of kneading.

Shape the dough into a ball, return it to the bowl, and cover. Leave in a warm place for about 60 minutes, until it has roughly doubled. Malt-enriched doughs can take longer than plain doughs; if your kitchen is cool, allow up to 90 minutes.

Grease the loaf tin. Knock back the dough, shape it into an oval to fit the tin, and lower it in seam-side down. Cover loosely and leave for 30 minutes, until the dough has risen to just above the rim of the tin.

Heat the oven to 160°C fan (170°C conventional). Bake for 50 to 55 minutes, until the loaf is deep mahogany brown, has pulled away slightly from the sides of the tin, and an instant-read thermometer inserted into the centre reads 96°C. Brush the hot top with a little malt extract dissolved in a tablespoon of water for a sticky glaze. Turn out and cool completely on a wire rack, then wrap tightly in cling film.
Slice thinly and serve with cold salted butter. The loaf keeps for up to a week wrapped at room temperature.
